clinical_history: A 67-year-old man. What is the QRS duration?  What is going on?
diagnosis: The QRS duration is normal.  What may appear to be a wide QRS in leads V2-V6 is actually massive ST elevation due to acute transmural anterior ischemia/myocardial infarction (MI) here.  There is reciprocal inferior lead ST depression.  Q waves are starting to appear in the precordial leads.  This ST pattern, called "tombstones" in the earthy parlance of the housestaff, is more technically known as a "monophasic current of injury."  This is also the pattern you see if you touch the epicardium of the heart with a needle (e.g. during pericardiocentesis).  Sometimes this pattern is mistaken for a bundle branch block. The left anterior descending (LAD) was occluded here and was opened with emergency catheterization and a "precutaneous transluminal et (PTCA) procedure" was the therapy of choice in this setting.

report.intervals: PR: ~160 ms, QRS: Normal (< 120 ms), QTc: N/A (prolonged due to massive ST segment distortion)
report.findings: Massive 'tombstone' ST-segment elevation in leads V2-V6, I, and aVL (monophasic current of injury); Reciprocal ST-segment depression in inferior leads II, III, and aVF; Early Q wave formation in precordial leads.
report.impression: Acute transmural anterior myocardial infarction (STEMI) due to left anterior descending (LAD) coronary artery occlusion.
